---
title: "Class 09"
author: "Yasmeen Al Shakarchi (PID: A18116120)"
format: pdf
---


## Background

In today's mini project we will analyze candy data with the exploratory graphics, basic statistics, correlation anaylysis and principal component analysis methods we have been learing thus far.

## Data Import

```{r}
candy <- read.csv("candy-data.csv", row.names = 1) 
head(candy)
```




> Q1. How many different candy types are in this dataset?

There are `r nrow(candy)` rows in this dataset

> Q2. How mant fruity candy types are in the dataset?

```{r}
sum(candy$fruity)
```

> Q3. What is your favorite candy (other than Twix) in the dataset and what is it’s winpercent value?

```{r}
candy["Air Heads", "winpercent"]
```




> Q4. What is the winpercent value for “Kit Kat”?

```{r}
candy["Kit Kat", "winpercent"]
```


> Q5. What is the winpercent value for “Tootsie Roll Snack Bars”?

```{r}
candy["Tootsie Roll Snack Bars", "winpercent"]
```


## Exploratory Analysis

> Q6. Is there any variable/column that looks to be on a different scale to the majority of the other columns in the dataset?


>Q7. What do you think a zero and one represent for the candy$chocolate column?


```{r}
library("skimr")
skim(candy)
```

>Q8. Plot a histogram of winpercent values using both base R an ggplot2.

```{r}
hist( candy$winpercent, breaks= 8)
```


```{r}
library(ggplot2) 

ggplot(candy) +
  aes(winpercent) +
  geom_histogram(bins= 12, fill = "lightblue", col= "darkgrey")
```


>Q9. Is the distribution of winpercent values symmetrical?

Answer: No it does not look symmetrical

>Q10. Is the center of the distribution above or below 50%?

```{r}
mean(candy$winpercent)
```

```{r}
summary(candy$winpercent)
```



Answer: It is above 50%. It is about 50.32%. 


```{r}
ggplot(candy) +
  aes(winpercent) +
  geom_boxplot()
```

>Q11. On average is chocolate candy higher or lower ranked than fruit candy?

Answer: The chocolate candy is liked more than the fruit candy. 

Steps to solve this: 

1. Find all chocolate candy in the dataset
2.Extract or find their winpercent values
3. Calculate the mean of these values
4. Find all fruit candy 
5. Find thier winpercent values
6. Calculate their mean of these values

step 1: 
```{r}
candy[ candy$chocolate==1, ]
```

step 2: 

```{r}
choc.candy <- candy[ candy$chocolate==1, ]
choc.win <- choc.candy$winpercent

```

step 3: 

```{r}
choc.candy <- candy[ candy$chocolate==1, ]
choc.win <- choc.candy$winpercent
mean(choc.win)
```

step 4-6: 
```{r}
fruit.candy <- candy[ candy$fruity==1, ] 
fruit.win <- fruit.candy$winpercent
mean(fruit.win)

```


>Q12. Is this difference statistically significant?

```{r}
t.test(choc.win, fruit.win)
```
Answer: Yes it is statistically significant because the p-value is less than 0.05. 

## Overall Candy Rankings

> Q13. What are the five least liked candy types in this set?

```{r}
y <- c("z", "c", "a")
sort(y)
```

```{r}
sort(candy$winpercent)
```


```{r}
y 
order(y)
```

```{r}
y [ order(y) ]
```


```{r}
inds <- order(candy$winpercent)
candy[inds, ]
```


```{r}
inds <- order(candy$winpercent)
candy[inds, ]
head( candy[inds, 5] )
```
Answer: Nik L Nip, Boston Baked Beans, Chiclets, Super Bubble, and Jawbusters


> Q14. What are the top 5 all time favorite candy types out of this set?


```{r}
tail(candy[inds, ], 5)
```

Answer: Snickers, Kit Kat, Twix, Reese's miniatures, and Reese's Peanut Butter cups.


>Q15. Make a first barplot of candy ranking based on winpercent values.

 This first graph is to hard to read and will be too small to read when rendering:
```{r}
ggplot(candy) +
  aes(winpercent, rownames(candy)) +
  geom_col()
```
Then we can change it to: 


```{r}
ggplot(candy) +
  aes(winpercent, rownames(candy)) +
  geom_col() +
  ylab("") # turn off Y-label that we don't need

ggsave("barplot1.png", height=10, width=6)
```
![]("barplot1.png")

> Q16. This is quite ugly, use the reorder() function to get the bars sorted by winpercent?

```{r}
ggplot(candy) +
  aes(winpercent,
    reorder( rownames(candy), winpercent) ) +
  geom_col() +
  ylab("") # turn off Y-label that we don't need

ggsave("barplot2.png", height=10, width=6)
```

![My second barplot for Q16](barplot2.png)




```{r}

ggplot(candy) +
  aes(winpercent, 
      reorder( rownames(candy), winpercent), 
      fill=chocolate) +
  geom_col() +
  ylab("") #turn of Y-label that we don't need

ggsave("barplot1.png", height=10, width = 6)
```


I want custom colors that I pick so we need to make this ourseleves...

```{r}
my_cols <- rep("black", nrow(candy))
my_cols[candy$chocolate==1] <- "chocolate"
my_cols[candy$bar==1] <- "lightblue"
my_cols[candy$fruity==1] <- "pink"
my_cols
```

```{r}
ggplot(candy) +
  aes(winpercent,
    reorder( rownames(candy), winpercent) ) +
  geom_col(fill = my_cols)
```


>Q17. What is the worst ranked chocolate candy?

Answer: Chiclets

>Q18. What is the best ranked fruity candy?

Answer: Starburst

## Taking a look at pricepercent

Make a plot of winpercent vs the pricepercent


```{r}
my_cols[as.logical(candy$fruity)] = "red"

ggplot(candy) + 
  aes(x=winpercent, y=pricepercent, label= rownames(candy)) +
  geom_point(col= my_cols) +
  geom_text(col= my_cols)
```

 
We can use the **ggrepel** package for better label placement:


```{r}
library(ggrepel)

ggplot(candy) + 
  aes(x=winpercent, y=pricepercent, label= rownames(candy)) +
  geom_point(col= my_cols) +
  geom_text_repel(col= my_cols, max.overlaps=7, size= 3.3)
```

>Q19. Which candy type is the highest ranked in terms of winpercent for the least money - i.e. offers the most bang for your buck?

```{r}
ord <- order(candy$pricepercent, decreasing = FALSE)
head(candy[ord, c(11,12)], n=5)
```

Answer: Tootsie Roll Midgies




>Q20. What are the top 5 most expensive candy types in the dataset and of these which is the least popular?


```{r}
ord <- order(candy$pricepercent, decreasing = TRUE)
head( candy[ord,c(11,12)], n=5 )
```
 Answer: Nik L Nip, Nestle Smarties, Ring pop, Hershey's Krackel, and Hershey's Milk Chocolate. The least popular one of these is Nik L Nip. 



## Exploring the correlation structure

Pearson correlation values range form -1 to +1

```{r}
library(corrplot)

cij <- cor(candy)
corrplot(cij)
```


>Q22. Examining this plot what two variables are anti-correlated (i.e. have minus values)?
 
 Answer: Fruity tends to not have nougat, and it is not penutyalmondy ( chocolate tends to be).


> Q23. Similarly, what two variables are most positively correlated?

Answer: Chocolate tends to be a bar and tends to be expensive and have more nougat than fruity.


## Principle Component Analysis

```{r}
pca <- prcomp(candy, scale=T)
summary(pca)
```


The main results figure: the PCA score plot:

```{r}
ggplot(pca$x)+
  aes(PC1, PC2, labels= rownames(pca$x)) +
  geom_point(col=my_cols) +
  geom_text_repel(col=my_cols) +
  labs(title= "PCA Candy Space Map", 
       subtitle= "Seperation")
```


```{r}
p <- ggplot(pca$x) +
  aes(PC1, PC2, label=rownames(pca$x)) +
  geom_point(col=my_cols) +
  geom_text_repel(col=my_cols) +
  labs(title ="PCA Candy Space map")
p
```

```{r}
#library(plotly)
#ggplotly(p)
```


The "loadings" plot for PC1

```{r}
ggplot(pca$rotation) +
  aes(PC1,
      reorder( rownames(pca$rotation), PC1) ) +
  geom_col() +
    ylab("")
```


> Q24. Complete the code to generate the loadings plot above. What original variables are picked up strongly by PC1 in the positive direction? Do these make sense to you? Where did you see this relationship highlighted previously?

Answer: In the plot the fruity, pluribus, and hard are the variables that are picked up strongly by PC1 in the positive direction. Yes this makes sense because it was seen in the eailer data and it captured that same divide:
fruity/hard/pluribus vs. chocolate/bar-type candies ( was seen in question 11). 

 
>Q25. Based on your exploratory analysis, correlation findings, and PCA results, what combination of characteristics appears to make a “winning” candy? How do these different analyses (visualization, correlation, PCA) support or complement each other in reaching this conclusion? 
 
Combination of characteristics appears to make a “winning” candy: chocolate, bar and nougat

The chocolate candy cluster at higher winpercent values and these features mentioned above show positive correlations with winpercent. Also on PC1 these are associated with popular candies.
